title: 10 best herbal creams for enlarged spleen

remedy_properties: Anti-inflammatory property: Turmeric's anti-inflammatory compound, curcumin, helps reduce inflammation and swelling in the spleen, alleviating symptoms associated with an enlarged spleen.

Antioxidant property: The antioxidant properties of turmeric cream help protect the spleen from oxidative stress and damage caused by free radicals, which can exacerbate conditions like an enlarged spleen.

Immune-modulating property: Turmeric's active compounds, particularly curcumin, have been shown to modulate immune function and reduce inflammation in the spleen, helping to alleviate symptoms of an enlarged spleen such as pain and discomfort.
remedies_list.remedy_desc: Curcuma longa, also known as turmeric, creams helps with enlarged spleen because of its potent anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties. The active compound, curcumin, present in turmeric has been shown to reduce inflammation and oxidative stress in the body. This helps in shrinking the spleen and reducing its workload. Additionally, curcumin also has immunomodulatory effects, which can help regulate the spleen's function and promote overall health. As a result, turmeric creams can be a natural and effective remedy for treating an enlarged spleen.
remedy_constituents: Curcumin: A polyphenolic compound that helps reduce inflammation and oxidative stress, which can contribute to the enlargement of the spleen by alleviating the underlying causes of spleen enlargement, such as infections or autoimmune disorders.

Demethoxycurcumin (DMC): A curcuminoid with potent anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties, which can help reduce the swelling and inflammation of the spleen, thereby alleviating its enlargement.

Volatile oils (Turmerones): A mixture of sesquiterpenes and other volatile compounds that possess anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties, which can help reduce the inflammation and oxidative stress that may contribute to spleen enlargement.
remedy_parts: Roots: The roots of Curcuma longa also contain curcumin, making them a suitable alternative for treating spleen-related issues.

Stems: The stems of Curcuma longa, although less commonly used, may also be employed in creams for enlarged spleen due to their potential anti-inflammatory properties, although more research is needed to confirm this.
remedy_recipe: Infuse curcuma longa rhizomes in a carrier oil such as sweet almond oil for 2-3 weeks.
Strain the infused oil through a cheesecloth to remove the solids and reserve the liquid.
Mix the infused oil with shea butter in a ratio of 1:1 and heat it gently.
Whip the mixture until it becomes light and fluffy adding a small amount of distilled water.
Pour the whipped mixture into a glass jar and store it in the refrigerator for up to 6 months.

remedy_recipe: Infuse 50 grams of dried root in 500 milliliters of carrier oil at 80 degrees celsius for 2 hours.
Strain the infused oil through a cheesecloth into a clean glass container and discard the solids.
Combine 250 milliliters of distilled water and 25 grams of beeswax in a double boiler and heat until melted.
Add 100 milliliters of the infused oil to the melted beeswax mixture and stir until well combined.
Pour the mixture into sterilized jars and allow to cool and solidify for 30 minutes.

remedy_recipe: Harvest 250 grams of allium sativum bulbs when the tops begin to yellow indicating maturity.
Chop the allium sativum bulbs into small pieces and dry them in a single layer for 7 days.
Combine 10 grams of dried allium sativum pieces with 50 grams of vegetable oil in a double boiler.
Heat the mixture in the double boiler for 2 hours while stirring occasionally to create a thick paste.
Mix 20 grams of beeswax with 20 grams of distilled water to create a thickening agent then combine with the allium sativum paste.

Heat the mixture over low heat for 10-15 minutes or until the powder dissolves completely.
Strain the mixture through a cheesecloth or a fine-mesh sieve to remove any sediment.
Add 2 tablespoons of a carrier oil such as coconut oil or sweet almond oil to the mixture.
Whip the mixture with a hand mixer or a whisk until it thickens into a creamy texture.

Heat the mixture over low heat for 10 minutes then let it steep for 20 minutes.
Strain the mixture through a cheesecloth into a bowl to separate the liquid from the solids.
Mix 1 tablespoon of beeswax with 2 tablespoons of shea butter in a small saucepan over low heat.
Blend 100ml of the cooled liquid from step 3 with the mixture from step 4 to create the cream.

remedy_recipe: Harvest and dry sufficient amounts of slippery elm bark, approximately 20 grams.
Grind the dried slippery elm bark into a fine powder using a spice grinder or mortar and pestle.
Combine the powdered slippery elm with 100 milliliters of coconut oil and 20 milliliters of beeswax in a double boiler.
Heat the mixture over low heat for approximately 30 minutes, stirring occasionally, until the beeswax is fully melted and the mixture is smooth.
Remove from heat, let cool to room temperature, then whip the mixture into a creamy consistency using a hand mixer or whisk.

remedy_recipe: Harvest fresh trifolium pratense flowers and leaves, ensuring sufficient quantity for subsequent processing.

remedy_recipe: Harvest 20 grams of dried valeriana officinalis roots and crush them into fine powder using a mortar and pestle for 10 minutes.
Mix 100 milliliters of cold-pressed sweet almond oil with 5 grams of the crushed valerian root powder and heat it in a double boiler at 60°c for 30 minutes.
Combine 20 grams of beeswax and 50 grams of shea butter in a separate double boiler and melt them together at 70°c for 15 minutes.
Slowly pour the melted oil mixture into the melted wax mixture while stirring constantly for 5 minutes, then remove from heat and let it cool to 40°c.
Pour the cooled cream mixture into sterilized jars and store in the refrigerator at a consistent temperature below 10°c for up to six months before use.

remedy_recipe: Gather organic silybum marianum dried flowers, coconut oil, beeswax, and vitamin e oil for the cream base preparation.
Mix 100g of dried silybum marianum flowers with 500ml of boiling water to create a decoction that will steep for 30 minutes.
Strain the decoction through cheesecloth into a clean glass container and discard the solids, then combine the liquid with 200g of melted coconut oil and stir well.
Add 20g of beeswax and 5ml of vitamin e oil to the mixture and heat it gently in a double boiler until the wax melts and the mixture thickens.
Pour the final cream into sterilized glass jars, seal them tightly, and store them in the refrigerator for up to six months or freeze for longer preservation.
